items.title: Autism subtypes identified using cross-species functional connectivity analyses
items.author: Alessandro Gozzi
items.content: Pagani et al. used cross-species fMRI to reveal two autism subtypes, characterized by lower and higher brain connectivity and linked to synaptic and immune-related pathways, respectively.

items.title: Excitatory synapses onto axonic spines jump-start action potentials and route information flow
items.author: Yousheng Shu
items.content: The axon initial segment was known to receive GABAergic synaptic inputs. Yang et al. show that it can be excited directly via specialized ‘axonic spines’. These spines thus boost neuronal firing and act as a fast track to route circuit information.

items.title: Parsing autism spectrum heterogeneity through fMRI
items.author: Yen-Yu Ian Shih
items.content: Autism is remarkably heterogeneous, posing a long-standing challenge for linking genetics to brain dynamics. A cross-species study identifies two principal dysconnectivity signatures across 20 mouse models of autism risk, each associated with distinct molecular pathways, and shows analogous connectivity patterns in autistic humans. These results establish a translational framework for biologically grounded fMRI phenotyping.

items.title: Synchronous climbing fiber activity enables instructive signaling for cerebellar learning through modulation of disinhibitory circuits
items.author: Jinseop S. Kim
items.content: Combining connectomics, physiology and behavior, this study shows how the cerebellum decides when to learn. Synchronized climbing-fiber error signals lift an inhibitory signal gate on Purkinje cells, enabling synaptic plasticity and motor adaptation.

items.title: Integrated single-cell and spatial transcriptomic profiling in ALS uncovers peripheral-to-central immune infiltration and reprogramming
items.author: David Gate
items.content: This study reveals that the immune system has a role in driving ALS. These findings link blood immune changes to spinal cord damage and suggest personalized treatments targeting specific immune pathways.

items.title: Closed-loop stimulation modulates attention shifting in children
items.author: George M. Ibrahim
items.content: Intracranial recordings in children revealed neural signals predicting attentional lapses. Closed-loop stimulation rescued performance and scalp EEG enabled noninvasive modulation, identifying a reproducible marker of attentional control.

items.title: Fluctuating internal states mediate neural–behavioral covariations in V1
items.author: Eyal Seidemann
items.content: The authors report surprising covariations between subthreshold activity of single V1 neurons and behavior, demonstrating that interactions between external sensory inputs and internal states are implemented by a nonlinear modulatory signal in V1.

items.title: Optics-free spatial genomics for mapping mammalian brain aging by IRISeq
items.author: Junyue Cao
items.content: The authors developed an optics-free spatial genomics method to map mouse brain aging, revealing region-specific inflammation and showing that lymphocytes promote harmful interferon signaling, whereas their loss preserves ependymal cells and reshapes glial states.
